summary: Joel Spolsky presents his comprehensive guide to interviewing programmers, arguing that hiring is the most critical factor in software project success. He contends that interviewers should evaluate candidates on exactly two criteria: being smart and getting things done. The guide advocates for a rigorous process with multiple interviewers, strict hire/no-hire decisions with no maybes, and a strong bias toward rejecting uncertain candidates. Spolsky recommends using open-ended coding problems (especially involving recursion and pointers) to assess aptitude rather than trivia questions or brain teasers. He emphasizes that rejecting a good candidate is far less costly than hiring a bad one, and that interviewers should look for passion, clear communication, and leadership in candidates' discussions of past work.
key_points: Hiring decisions should be binary (Hire or No Hire) with no maybes — any uncertainty should mechanically translate to No Hire, because a bad hire is far more costly than a missed good candidate.
The only two qualities that matter in a programming candidate are being Smart and Getting Things Done — people who have only one of these traits are net negatives for a team.
Interview coding problems should test aptitude (recursion, pointers, multiple levels of abstraction) rather than trivia knowledge or specific technology skills, which become obsolete quickly.
Multiple interviewers (at least six, mostly peers) should evaluate each candidate independently to avoid groupthink and bias, with any two no-hires being sufficient to reject.
During open-ended discussion, interviewers should look for passion, ability to explain concepts clearly to non-experts, and evidence of past leadership and initiative.
money_quotes: The trick is telling the difference between the superstars and the maybes, because the secret is that you don't want to hire any of the maybes. Ever.
It is much, much better to reject a good candidate than to accept a bad candidate.
If you can't tell, that means No Hire. It's really easier than you'd think. Can't tell? Just say no!
Smart does not mean 'knows the answer to trivia questions.' Software teams want to hire people with aptitude, not a particular skill set.
For some reason most people seem to be born without the part of the brain that understands pointers.
If the basic concepts aren't so easy that you don't even have to think about them, you're not going to get the big concepts.
People who Get Things Done but are not Smart will do stupid things, seemingly without thinking about them, and somebody else will have to come clean up their mess later.
I want my ER doctor to understand anatomy, even if all she has to do is put the computerized defibrillator nodes on my chest and push the big red button.
key_insight: Great software teams are built by hiring only people who are both smart and get things done, with a deliberate bias toward rejecting uncertain candidates rather than risking a bad hire.
